attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old woman presents to the gynaecology clinic with a three-week history of vaginal bleeding. She underwent menopause at age 51. She has had three children, all by vaginal deliveries. She has a medical history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Her medications include metformin and perindopril. Her body mass index is 45 kg/m2.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an endometrial thickness of 15 mm (less than 4). Endometrial pipelle biopsy reveals grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma. Magnetic resonance imaging shows no evidence of myometrial invasion or lymph node involvement.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Total laparoscopic hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy by gynaecological oncologist
Total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy by general gynaecologist
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with post-menopausal bleeding and is found to have early-stage endometrial cancer confirmed by endometrial biopsy. The standard surgical treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer is total hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. Total laparoscopic hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy by gynaecological oncologist is the most appropriate management as it offers the benefits of minimally invasive surgery while ensuring optimal oncological outcomes.

Vaginal hysterectomy by general gynaecologist would not allow adequate assessment of the upper abdomen and potential spread of disease. Furthermore, gynaecological cancer surgery should be performed by subspecialty-trained gynaecological oncologists who can perform appropriate staging if necessary. Vaginal hysterectomy also usually makes access difficult to perform a bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy which should also be performed for staging in endometrial carcinoma procedures.

Total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy by general gynaecologist is not appropriate as endometrial cancer should be managed by specialists with expertise in gynaecological oncology. Additionally, when feasible, minimally invasive approaches are preferred over open surgery due to reduced post-operative pain, shorter hospital stay, and faster recovery.

Radiation therapy alone is not appropriate as primary treatment for operable early-stage endometrial cancer. While radiation may be used as adjuvant therapy in some cases, surgery remains the primary treatment modality.

Progestogen therapy alone may be considered in young women with early-stage, low-grade disease who wish to preserve fertility, or in patients who are medically unfit for surgery. However, this patient is post-menopausal and appears medically fit for surgery, making hormonal therapy inappropriate as primary treatment.
learningPoints: Total laparoscopic hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy is the primary surgical treatment for early-stage endometrial cancer.
Referral to a gynaecological oncologist is essential for optimal surgical outcomes in endometrial cancer.
Minimally invasive surgery is preferred over open surgery for early-stage endometrial cancer due to reduced morbidity and faster recovery.
references: Mahdy H, Casey MJ, Vadakekut ES, Crotzer D. Endometrial Cancer [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 [cited 2024 Nov 24]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK525981/
